sentences.text: Later mother told me [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] that father wanted to committed suicide.
She didn't let him.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I mean, not that he, he had something in his hands or, or, or poison or something, but he was talking about it.
So grandfather was watching over him.
And I remember when he wanted to cut his nails, he didn't let him do it because he was so afraid.
My father was 52 on the 13th of May 1944.
Mother was 44 on the 2nd of May 1944.
My grandfather was in his late or near late 60s, grandmother, probably a few years younger.
And my Uncle Nandor, I believe, was in his late 50s-- mid to late 50s.
My aunt, who is here, was 30-- let's see, she was 38.
In the ghetto, it was-- it was vegetation.
That's what it was.
They-- in somebody's apartment, they, they put up a Jewish hospital, and that's where I went every day to scrub the floor, and do the cleaning, and do whatever I could.
I volunteered to do it.
I, I felt that if I could help, I was young and I was strong, I could contribute something to, to-- for the sake of the sick.
By that time there were no businesses-- no, nothing.
Everything was taken away or, or closed, closed.
Not taken away but closed, locked up.
You couldn't-- you couldn't have business.
You couldn't write.
You couldn't do anything.
One of my uncles-- this aunt's husband-- Uncle Laszlo-- he was a dentist, and they let him work on his own for a while.
But by then he was drafted into a forced labor battalion.
So were the two brothers of my mother.
They were not in the ghetto, but they were in Hungary.
Absolutely nothing, absolutely nothing.
You were waiting from one day to the other what will come next.
You, you, you-- we became-- it's apathy.
It's, it's when you-- when you suspect the worst, and even worse than the worst is happening.

And we knew that we couldn't stay at the ghetto, because gradually, in May, while we were in the ghetto, we found out that people on the border areas have been put on trains and shipped out, and we didn't know where.
And then one day we heard that postcards marked Waldsee, W-A-L-D-S-E-E, came saying we are in Waldsee, and we are working.
And it turned out, later we found out that by the time the postcards arrived, the very people who wrote the cards went up in smoke already in Auschwitz, or, or somewhere else.
Most of the people were shipped to Auschwitz from Hungary.
Nothing.
Absolutely nothing.
We heard the word Dachau as, as a political, political-- not even internment camp, some kind of a prison.
But the word concentration camp, we just didn't know what it is.
We heard once that in Lublin they, they, they harmed some Jews.
In, in Galicia.
In-- well then, I think it was occupied-- occupied Galicia or Poland.
Now it belongs to Russia I think.
I don't know.
But something is happening, but we didn't know for sure, and we didn't know the facts.
There were only rumors.
You were not supposed to-- you were not permitted to listen to the voice of America, which of course we have.
And we did until we went to the ghetto.
In the ghetto, we didn't have radio.
We didn't have nothing.
From one person to the other.

Well, we knew that we could not stay in the ghetto forever, but we didn't know what's going to happen because of course, the optimism-- the Jewish optimism that whatever happens in the border areas will not affect us because we are only at the Czechoslovakia border, and then that this is a friendly country, Czechoslovakia.
How wrong we were.
Until the 6th of June.
They, they-- I don't now whether it was by written degree-- decree-- or the military or the gendarmes came to tell in the ghetto what was going to happen.
That I don't remember.
But I know that we had to move out on the 6th of June, 1944.
And as we were walking to-- they said we have to go to a place which they called the gathering point, the concentration camp.
They didn't use the word concentration camp.
To camp, probably that's what they said.
And that's where we will stay, which, it turned out, it was a series of stables.
And the certain, certain Jewish men within the forced labor unit were cleaning the streets, and they were whispering to us that the Allied landed in Normandy.
Of course, we were elated, but it was late for us and late for the many millions.

And then we arrived to this stable place, and we had a choice of 'resting,' quote, unquote, on the hay-- on the remnants of the hay just left over by the horses and cows before-- or staying in the open stalls and exposed to the elements.
And we chose the latter.
That was on the 6th, and eventually they brought some more Jews into this camp from the surrounding towns.
And I believe on the 11th of June they herded us to the cattle cars, and they jammed 98 people-- men, women, and children and one empty pail-- in each car.
And I think we were just waiting there for the night because I believe it was the 12th of June that the train started to roll.
We didn't know where.
We knew it's to the north because we knew the surrounding area.
There were a few stops, like in the town Kassa, K-A-S-S-A.
They opened-- I don't know how many days it took us to go there.
It was not too far from Salgotarjan.

They-- of course, you were only supposed to take small-- not even bag-- a small something, like knapsack or something, and some, some food that you could hold in your hand and put it in the thing, in the sack.
Gendarme and the military.
Yes.
Yes.
I don't know, but probably the SS was guarding the place.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
But the Hungarians were just as bad, as evil as the SS.
You couldn't get any worse than the Hungar-- those Hungarians who collaborated.
Believe it or not, before we left the ghetto they subjected us to a gynecological search.
I saw my piano teacher standing in one room, and they took us behind the-- behind the screen.
And even my small cousins-- they were t-- they were-- they were in their teens, between eight and 12-- they were subjected to this.
I mean, how, how worse, how, how more degenerate can they get, all the Hungarians?
So up to this time, almost all of your contact was with the Hungarians--
The Hungarians.
--and not the Germans at all.
Most probably in the background it was the Germ-- I mean Germ-- Hungary was occupied by the Germans.
And then when we came back from the camp, and I met this piano teacher of mine, I ask him, Professor Vadaszi, V-A-D-A-S-Z-I, how could you do it?
And he told me something to the effect that, I did not have any other choice.
I don't remember his exact words, but this is just about what he said.
I could have killed him.
There were some-- there was some resistance.
I believe in town called Munkacs-- which belongs now to Russia, M-U-N-K-A-C-S-- that's what we were told, and that the people were all shot.
I am not sure whether we heard it before or after.
I am not sure, but I am positive I have heard it.
No.
No.
Certain people escaped.
Like for example, my cousin Vera's-- well, they were not engaged yet, but the friendship was getting serious.
And a young man escaped from the ghetto and went to Budapest.
And then they were hiding with the family, and the young man.
And then subsequently, they got married after the war.
As I said, in the town Kassa, the train stopped.
And whatever was left of our clothing-- I remember I had a raincoat that I got from my mother.
It was like, like very, very whitish green silk, like parachute silk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I had to give it to them, but before I-- before I, I, I had to-- I handed it over, and I had a free moment, I ripped it apart on the seam.
I mean, it was stupid, but it was just the-- I don't know what it was, but I had to do it.
He could have shot me bec-- for this.
I mean, it was a nonsensical thing to do, but I felt I have to do it.
I didn't want to give him my own belonging just because he asked for it.
This, this person, this Hungarian, this, this nobody who-- some auxiliary person 'attending,' quote, unquote, the train.
The stop was only good for one reason, that they should rob the Jews of the tiny, little things that they were left with after having been put on the convoy.
That was the only thing.
I don't remember.
Maybe they emptied the pail.
Excuse me, but that's how it went.
One pail for 98 people.
I don't remember they gave us water.
They may have, I don't remember.
And that's how it went.
My parents, my grandparents, the aunts, and the cousins.
On my mother's side because the br-- and my uncle my father's side because the other three uncles were drafted in the forced labor battalion.
I believe they put us on on the 11th.
I believe we spent the night there because I remember hearing the air raid sirens, and we were hoping that we will have a hit.
It would have been far better than to go through hell in Auschwitz, to die by Allied bombs.
And then the train pulled out, the convoy pulled out I believe on the 12th.
And we arrive in Auschwitz on the 15h of June.
It was a Thursday.
There is the arch that you see.
It's so recognizable.
I mean, how can one miss it?
And near the arch there was a station mark, as far as I can remember.
Maybe on the other side.
I don't know because then the train must have pulled to the side spur to Birkenau because it made it easier for them to put the people-- to take the people off the train and just separate everybody.
And then to the left was the Birkenau gas chamber and crematoria.
So instead of stopping at Auschwitz station, the train, after a while, pulled into Birkenau.
And I think that at Auschwitz station I must have seen a clock.
I believe-- well I can see the clock, but I cannot place it, where I saw it.
That's, that's the problem.
But I believe it showed 7:20 AM, I think.
But I'm positive it was on the 15th of June, 1944.
Yes, yes, yes.
They opened up the bolts, and just before they opened the bolts-- during the journey, somebody went into labor and was giving birth just as they unlocked the bolts.
And she was, along with the baby, the first person they took away.
All this upheaval on the station, and so many people.
And, and, and first we saw that they are what you would call here red caps.
The people in striped suits running around and yelling and screaming in German, get off the plane, leave everything there because it will be delivered to you later.
From then on, I think we became totally disoriented-- totally.
And as we got off the train and walked a few steps, they started separating us.
My aunt, Olga-- who is in New York-- was with Grandma.
I was with my father and my mother.
And the person separated Aunt Olga from Grandmother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father-- I can see them, they are here.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They were pushed to the left as we started walking.
My father, my uncle, my grandmother, my grandfather, the children, and my aunts were are all directed to the left.
My mother, my Aunt Olga, and I were directed to the right.
Not by Mengele, by a tall, rat-faced high ranking SS man, who turned out to be Heinz, Dr.
Heinz-- some doctor-- Dr.
Heinz Thilo.
It took me 30 years, I believe, 30 years to track him down through the Wiesenthal Center.
I wrote to Mr.
Wiesenthal, and he gave me the news that Thilo committed suicide on the day of the German surrender.
That's a hero for you.
Some hero.
So eventually they marched us to the-- I remember the last word of my father telling Mother, [NON-ENGLISH].
When you say [NON-ENGLISH], it means my cherie.
[NON-ENGLISH] they are separating us.
And that was the last time we heard his voice.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [CRYING] It's been 51 years, but it's like yesterday.

[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] So they marched us to the-- they, they said we should leave everything there because we will get it back, and we should be very careful how we put everything where so that we don't mess up the order.
And they took us to the-- oh yeah, they told us they after bath you will get water, and food, and all the nonsense.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we had to leave our clothing there.
The shoes sometimes could be retrieved, but there was some fetid looking water that they put the shoes in.
I don't know what kind of a disinfectant or something, or just part of torture, who knows.
So you were able to retrieve that.
And then they shaved all our body hair-- but I mean all of it.
And we were standing naked, s-- preceding-- proceeding from the shower through a long corridor, all naked, stark naked.
And we were waiting there.
We didn't know for what, but we noticed that to the right-- I can see it.
I mean it's here even after 51 years-- a huge hole with ovens, closed-- with ovens with closed doors, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] spotlessly clean.
I mean, there was nothing at all that you can figure out what's what.
We thought, it's, its a bakery.
Every door was closed.
No smudges, no nothing.
Some prisoners were standing there, which of course later we found out were the members of the Sonderkommando and some SS men.
Nobody uttered a word.
And little did we know that in a few hours, this will be the last depository of members of our family and the rest of the convoy people who were put to the-- who were herded to the left.
You know, again, I have to repeat myself.
It's been 51 years-- it will be 51 on the 15th of June.
It's happening now.
I see them.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then they herded us to yet another room where female prisoners with long brushes wiped us in the front and in the back with some kind of disinfectant, which of course, with the coarse brush, caused lacerations, and it was a burning, nasty sensation.
And they gave us some kind of a rag, and we were dismissed.
They took us-- oh, we couldn't figure out what the smoke is, or what-- eventually it got dark-- what the red flame.
We didn't have the slightest idea.
The smell, probably burning rubber.
It, it turned out that there was a synthetic rubber factory in Monowitz a couple of kilometers from Auschwitz where they had Auschwitz prisoners working there as slave laborers.
Totally disoriented, totally.
I mean, the, the normal perception of reality just cut off from one second to the other.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They took us to lager B3.
It was a number of meters-- I mean, a good number of meters from the man's camp, and behind the man's camp-- I'm talking about Birkenau, not Auschwitz-- from the gypsy camp.
As it turned out, we stayed in block 10, and then in block five.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was an extremely vicious blockalteste by the name of Mala, M-A-L-A.
The blockalteste was the woman-- the Jewish woman supervisor who was running that particular block where they put us up.
It was a series of small huts.
They jammed in at least 1,000 or 1,200-- I don't know how many women.
In block 10, or five, or anywhere in lager B3, there were no cots.
They made us lie on the floor.
I had my head on the rear end of the inmate before me, and on my rear end was resting the prisoner whose turn it was to be there.
So, if during the night somebody had to go to the latrine, couldn't find a place to sit down, less to sleep.
That was Birkenau.
I was with my mother in my aunt, and somehow-- I don't know what made us think-- from then on, I never ever called her mother.
I only called her Sari.
They did not know, except the people from our town, that we are relatives because it was not good to have a relative in Auschwitz.
I don't know who told us.
I don't know whether anybody ever told us.
But we saw at the-- on arrival that families are torn apart.
Probably that's what gave us the idea.
From our-- no, no. they were only Hungarians.
They said that this is the Hungarian-- this is the Hungarian series of blocks.
There were a number of blocks on both sides of the small road.
It was separated by small pebbles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- I mean, the, the two rows on both sides of the road-- I don't know how many blocks, 10, 15?
I don't know.
But at the very end there was a sort of a hospital, lazarette.
It called Revier in the camp, R-E-V-I-E-R.
That's how they called it.
I got very sick in Auschwitz after a number of weeks, and somebody from our town who somehow got a job as a chambermaid of one of the Jewish supervisors, suggested that Mother take me up there.
Maybe I get some medication.
